cases.description: Exact spherical sag for a positive radius. R = 50 mm, r = 1.000 mm.
Exact spherical sag for a negative radius. R = -50 mm, r = 1.000 mm.
cases.derivation: For R<0, the same sag magnitude (negative sign). Formula R – sign(R)*√(R²–r²) yields –r²/(2|R|) – higher terms = –0.0100010002 mm.

cases.description: Phase applied by a thin lens at an off‑axis point. f = 100 mm, x = 0.100 mm, λ = 0.500 µm.
cases.derivation: phase = –π x² / (λ f). λ = 0.0005 mm, so π·0.01 / (0.0005·100) = 0.2π = 0.6283185307 rad, with a negative sign per convention.

cases.description: Largest radius r on a spherical surface where the local phase step stays ≤ π rad. λ = 0.5 µm, dx = 0.001 mm, R = 20 mm, |Δn| = 0.5.
Effective focal length of a thick spherical lens. R1 = 50 mm, R2 = –50 mm, centre thickness 5 mm, n = 1.5.

Power transmitted by a circular aperture of radius 0.2 mm placed 10 mm after the waist of a Gaussian beam (w₀ = 0.3 mm, λ = 1.064 µm). At z=10 mm the beam radius is essentially w₀. A probe exactly at the aperture should capture the clipped field.
cases.derivation: Transmission T = 1 – exp(–2R²/w²) = 1 – exp(–2·0.04/0.09) = 1 – exp(–0.888889) = 1 – 0.411169 = 0.588831. (The discrete sampled power will approach this value on a fine grid; tolerance accounts for discretisation.)

cases.description: Forward 2D DFT of an 8×8 uniform field (all ones) yields the DC bin value.
cases.derivation: Unitary normalisation 1/√(nx·ny) gives DC = (nx·ny)/√(nx·ny) = √64 = 8. Imag part exactly 0 because input is purely real and completely symmetric. (Imaginary DC part is numerically ~1e-16 and asserted separately in unit tests.)

cases.description: Total optical path (reduced length) through a three‑surface stack. Surfaces: (t=5mm, n=1.5), (t=2mm, n=1.4), (t=0mm, n=1.0).
